news_summary: Scientists have uncovered new cosmic phenomena including star-powered neutrino factories, insights into black hole winds affecting galaxy star formation, and mysterious substances on Titan and Pluto.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont mis au jour de nouveaux phénomènes cosmiques, notamment des « usines à neutrinos » alimentées par des étoiles, des découvertes sur les vents émis par les trous noirs qui influencent la formation stellaire au sein des galaxies, ainsi que des substances mystérieuses présentes sur Titan et Pluton.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han descubierto nuevos fenómenos cósmicos, entre los que se incluyen «fábricas» de neutrinos alimentadas por estrellas, nuevos datos sobre los vientos de los agujeros negros que influyen en la formación estelar de las galaxias y sustancias misteriosas en Titán y Plutón.

articles.title: Scientists expected a black hole but found a neutrino factory powered by stars
articles.summary: A distant galaxy nicknamed Shadow Blaster may have revealed a surprising source of cosmic neutrinos: extreme star formation instead of a supermassive black hole. The discovery suggests that hidden, dust-filled starburst galaxies could account for a significant fraction of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos.
articles.content:
Astronomers using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) have tracked down the source of a powerful neutrino burst with the help of a remarkable cosmic phenomenon that acted like a natural telescope. What they discovered challenged expectations.

Researchers initially suspected that a supermassive black hole was powering an extraordinarily bright distant galaxy linked to the neutrino signal. Instead, observations revealed that the galaxy's energy comes from intense star formation. The finding provides important evidence that could help explain where many of the Universe's mysterious high-energy neutrinos originate.

Tracking One of the Universe's Most Elusive Particles

Neutrinos are among the most puzzling particles known to science. Vast numbers of them pass through space, and even through Earth, with very little interaction with matter. Although astronomers have identified a handful of galaxies capable of producing neutrinos, those known sources are not enough to account for the large population of high-energy neutrinos detected so far.

To investigate the origin of one such particle, an international team of researchers from MITOS Science Co., LTD., National Central University, Chung Yuan Christian University, Tohoku University, Fukui University of Technology, and the National Astronomical Observatory of Japan conducted follow-up observations using ALMA and several other telescopes.

Their target was the high-energy neutrino event IC 210922A, which was detected by the IceCube Neutrino Observatory at the South Pole. The search led them to an exceptionally luminous galaxy known as JCMT0402−0424, located roughly 11 billion light-years from Earth.

The Mystery of Shadow Blaster

Previously identified neutrino-producing galaxies have typically been powered by supermassive black holes. However, when the researchers examined JCMT0402−0424, they found no evidence of the energetic emissions normally associated with such a black hole.

The galaxy is heavily veiled by dust, making it difficult to see in visible light. At submillimeter wavelengths, however, it shines intensely. Because of its hidden nature and extreme brightness at those wavelengths, the team gave it the nickname 'Shadow Blaster.'

A Natural Telescope Reveals the Galaxy's Core

Astronomers were able to look deep inside Shadow Blaster thanks to a fortunate alignment with another galaxy positioned between it and Earth. The foreground galaxy's gravity bent and amplified radio waves coming from Shadow Blaster, effectively creating a natural telescope.

This gravitational lensing effect produced brighter and enlarged images that allowed ALMA to examine the distant galaxy in far greater detail.

The radio observations again showed no sign of a powerful black hole. Instead, the data pointed toward another source of energy. The gas and dust throughout the galaxy appear to be heated primarily by vigorous star formation.

Researchers also identified a dense "compact core" at the center of Shadow Blaster. Large quantities of gas and dust are packed into a region only about 1,500 light-years across. Such an extreme environment is capable of generating neutrinos.

A New Explanation for High-Energy Neutrinos

The results suggest that intense star-forming galaxies may represent an important and previously underappreciated source of high-energy neutrinos.

According to the team, compact, dust-rich starburst galaxies undergoing rapid star formation could contribute a substantial share of the high-energy neutrino background. Their analysis indicates that these galaxies may account for as much as 20% of the total population of high-energy neutrinos observed across the Universe.

If confirmed by future studies, the discovery could significantly reshape scientists' understanding of how some of the Universe's most elusive particles are produced.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about the origin of high-energy neutrinos, revealing that intense star formation in distant galaxies, rather than supermassive black holes, can produce these elusive particles. This breakthrough advances understanding of cosmic phenomena with broad implications for astrophysics and particle physics, supported by detailed observations and international collaboration.
articles.personality_title: Distant galaxy powered by star formation, not black hole, produces cosmic neutrinos
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered where high-energy neutrinos, tiny particles that pass through space and matter almost unnoticed, come from. Many thought supermassive black holes in distant galaxies were the main source.

**What happened** – Using a powerful telescope array called ALMA and natural cosmic magnification, researchers studied a faraway galaxy named Shadow Blaster, about 11 billion light-years away. Instead of finding a black hole, they discovered that intense star formation inside this dusty galaxy is creating the neutrinos.

**Impact** – This finding changes how scientists understand neutrino sources. It shows that starburst galaxies hidden by dust, not just black holes, can produce a large portion of the Universe’s high-energy neutrinos—possibly around 20% of them. This helps explain where many of these mysterious particles originate.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study more dust-filled starburst galaxies to see how common this neutrino production is. Confirming these results could reshape theories about cosmic particle creation and the role of star formation in the Universe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Intense star formation in distant, dusty galaxies can produce many high-energy neutrinos, offering a new explanation beyond black holes for these elusive cosmic particles.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une galaxie lointaine produit des neutrinos cosmiques grâce à la formation d’étoiles, pas à un trou noir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps d’où viennent les neutrinos à haute énergie, de minuscules particules qui traversent l’espace et la matière presque sans interaction. On pensait souvent que des trous noirs supermassifs dans des galaxies lointaines étaient leur principale source.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à un puissant réseau de télescopes appelé ALMA et à un effet naturel d’amplification cosmique, les chercheurs ont étudié une galaxie très éloignée surnommée Shadow Blaster, située à environ 11 milliards d’années-lumière. Au lieu de trouver un trou noir, ils ont découvert que la formation intense d’étoiles dans cette galaxie poussiéreuse produit les neutrinos.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la compréhension des sources de neutrinos. Elle montre que les galaxies en pleine formation d’étoiles, souvent cachées par la poussière, peuvent produire une grande partie des neutrinos à haute énergie de l’Univers — environ 20 % d’entre eux. Cela aide à expliquer l’origine de nombreuses particules mystérieuses.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier d’autres galaxies poussiéreuses en formation d’étoiles pour vérifier si cette production de neutrinos est courante. Confirmer ces résultats pourrait modifier les théories sur la création des particules cosmiques et le rôle de la formation d’étoiles dans l’Univers.

**En une phrase** – La formation intense d’étoiles dans des galaxies lointaines et poussiéreuses peut produire de nombreux neutrinos à haute énergie, offrant une nouvelle explication au-delà des trous noirs pour ces particules cosmiques difficiles à détecter.
articles.personality_title_es: Galaxia lejana produce neutrinos cósmicos gracias a la formación de estrellas, no a un agujero negro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado durante mucho tiempo de dónde provienen los neutrinos de alta energía, partículas muy pequeñas que atraviesan el espacio y la materia casi sin interactuar. Muchos pensaban que los agujeros negros supermasivos en galaxias distantes eran la fuente principal.

**Qué pasó** – Usando un potente conjunto de telescopios llamado ALMA y un efecto natural de aumento cósmico, los investigadores estudiaron una galaxia muy lejana llamada Shadow Blaster, a unos 11 mil millones de años luz. En lugar de encontrar un agujero negro, descubrieron que la intensa formación de estrellas dentro de esta galaxia polvorienta está creando los neutrinos.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia cómo los científicos entienden las fuentes de neutrinos. Muestra que las galaxias con formación rápida de estrellas, ocultas por polvo, pueden producir gran parte de los neutrinos de alta energía del Universo, posiblemente alrededor del 20%. Esto ayuda a explicar el origen de muchas de estas partículas misteriosas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar más galaxias polvorientas con formación intensa de estrellas para ver qué tan común es esta producción de neutrinos. Confirmar estos resultados podría cambiar teorías sobre la creación de partículas cósmicas y el papel de la formación estelar en el Universo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La intensa formación de estrellas en galaxias distantes y polvorientas puede producir muchos neutrinos de alta energía, ofreciendo una nueva explicación más allá de los agujeros negros para estas esquivas partículas cósmicas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a luminous, dust-shrouded distant galaxy glowing softly in rich amber and deep reds, with swirling clouds of gas and dust forming a dense compact core at its center, surrounded by delicate arcs of light bending around a subtle, ghostly silhouette of a foreground galaxy representing gravitational lensing, all set against a dark cosmic background speckled with gentle stars, symbolizing intense star formation powering the galaxy and the natural cosmic telescope revealing its hidden neutrino factory.

articles.title: Black hole winds may be robbing giant galaxies of their future stars
articles.summary: Astronomers may be closing in on a long-standing cosmic mystery: why some of the universe’s biggest galaxies seem to have far fewer stars than expected. Using NASA- and JAXA-supported XRISM observations of a galaxy called NGC 4151, researchers found strong evidence that supermassive black holes can unleash powerful winds that blow away the raw material needed to make new stars.
articles.content:
Astronomers may be getting closer to solving a long-standing mystery about the universe's largest galaxies. Observations from the X-Ray Imaging and Spectroscopy Mission, known as XRISM, are providing new evidence that supermassive black holes could be preventing these giant galaxies from forming as many stars as expected.

According to current models, the most massive galaxies should contain more stellar mass than astronomers actually observe. The shortfall suggests that some process has been suppressing star formation. University of Michigan doctoral student Xin "Cindy" Xiang has used XRISM data to investigate one leading explanation and found evidence pointing directly to black holes.

Most people know black holes as objects whose gravity is so strong that even light cannot escape once it crosses a certain boundary. However, black holes can also create extremely bright regions around themselves. As gas and dust spiral inward, they form an accretion disk that emits enormous amounts of energy, including powerful X-rays.

Black Hole Winds and Star Formation

Accretion disks are among the most energetic environments in the universe. Material falling toward the black hole is heated by gravity and friction until it becomes an intensely hot plasma. At the same time, the disk can launch powerful outflows of matter.

These winds can be strong enough to sweep gas out of a galaxy. Because gas is the raw material needed to make new stars, such outflows could significantly reduce future star formation.

Data from XRISM support that possibility. The mission is led by the Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency in partnership with NASA and the European Space Agency.

"Previously, without XRISM, we could only see broad features of the outflows," Xiang said. "But you need to be able to resolve fine features to answer important questions. What is their structure and geometry? How are the winds launched and when are they launched?"

XRISM Delivers a Sharper View

Launched in 2023, XRISM began scientific observations in fall 2024. Its energy resolution is roughly 10 times better than that of its predecessor, allowing astronomers to examine black hole environments in far greater detail.

Xiang and her collaborators have focused on NGC 4151, a bright galaxy located a little more than 50 million light-years from Earth. At its center is an active galactic nucleus, or AGN, where a supermassive black hole is actively consuming material and generating a luminous accretion disk. This makes NGC 4151 an ideal laboratory for studying black hole driven outflows.

"With XRISM, we have the greatest resolution observing the brightest AGN and we're getting the richest information on outflows that we have observed so far for an accretion disk," Xiang said.

Working alongside University of Michigan astronomy professor Jon Miller, Xiang previously showed that winds from NGC 4151's accretion disk can reach speeds high enough to eject material from the system. She also identified the likely mechanism driving these outflows (that appears to be what's called magnetocentrifugal driving and it's similar to what sets off solar flares).

Tracking the Fastest Black Hole Outflows

At the 248th meeting of the American Astronomical Society in Pasadena, California, Xiang presented a new method for determining when NGC 4151's powerful winds are active. The approach could help researchers identify similar outflows in other galaxies and improve understanding of AGNs throughout the universe.

Because AGN winds can change dramatically over time, Xiang needed a way to pinpoint when the fastest and strongest outflows occurred. To do this, she analyzed hundreds of days of XRISM observations of NGC 4151.

Her work focused on periods when the galaxy's X-ray output brightened in flares and on how the X-ray signal evolved in the hours afterward.

In addition to measuring brightness, Xiang studied whether the detected X-rays were relatively hard or soft, a property comparable to color in visible light. She combined these measurements into a new metric called the color intensity index. Miller suggested shortening the name to "cindicity."

"Partly because my name is Cindy," Xiang said. "But the idea is that, in the future, you could tell me the cindicity of your source at this moment and I can tell you the probability that you're seeing a fast outflow."

A New Timing Link Between Black Holes and Galactic Winds

The analysis revealed a surprising pattern. In NGC 4151, the strongest fast winds appeared when the X-rays were hard but relatively faint.

The fastest outflows did not occur during the X-ray flares themselves. Instead, they typically appeared about 10,000 seconds, or just under three hours, later. This finding provides the first direct timing connection between X-ray activity and the powerful winds flowing from the black hole's accretion disk.

By identifying when these outflows occur, astronomers now have a valuable new tool for studying how black holes influence the growth and evolution of galaxies, and possibly why some of the universe's most massive galaxies are missing so many stars.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in understanding how supermassive black holes influence galaxy evolution by suppressing star formation through powerful winds. This discovery, enabled by advanced XRISM observations, has broad implications for astrophysics and our knowledge of the universe, representing a meaningful advancement with clear context and detailed explanation.
articles.personality_title: New observations reveal how black hole winds slow star formation in giant galaxies
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered why the biggest galaxies in the universe have fewer stars than expected. One idea is that supermassive black holes at the center of these galaxies might be stopping new stars from forming.

**What happened** – Using data from XRISM, a new space telescope launched in 2023, researchers studied a galaxy called NGC 4151, about 50 million light-years away. They found strong evidence that winds coming from the area around the galaxy’s central black hole blow away the gas needed to create new stars. These winds move fast and can push gas out of the galaxy.

**Impact** – This discovery helps explain why some giant galaxies don’t have as many stars as scientists predicted. By linking the timing of X-ray signals from the black hole to the strong winds, researchers now better understand how black holes can control star formation. This is a big step in learning how galaxies grow and change over time.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use this new method of tracking black hole winds in other galaxies. This will help them see if similar processes happen elsewhere and improve our knowledge of how black holes shape the universe.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New detailed observations show that powerful winds from black holes can blow away star-making gas, explaining why some giant galaxies have fewer stars than expected.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles observations montrent comment les vents des trous noirs freinent la formation d’étoiles dans les grandes galaxies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps pourquoi les plus grandes galaxies de l’univers ont moins d’étoiles que prévu. Une idée est que les trous noirs supermassifs au centre de ces galaxies pourraient empêcher la formation de nouvelles étoiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce aux données de XRISM, un nouveau télescope spatial lancé en 2023, des chercheurs ont étudié une galaxie appelée NGC 4151, située à environ 50 millions d’années-lumière. Ils ont trouvé des preuves solides que des vents provenant de la région autour du trou noir central de la galaxie chassent le gaz nécessaire à la création de nouvelles étoiles. Ces vents sont rapides et peuvent expulser le gaz hors de la galaxie.

**Impact** – Cette découverte aide à expliquer pourquoi certaines grandes galaxies ont moins d’étoiles que ce que les scientifiques prévoyaient. En reliant le moment où les rayons X sont émis par le trou noir aux vents puissants, les chercheurs comprennent mieux comment les trous noirs peuvent contrôler la formation d’étoiles. C’est un grand pas pour comprendre comment les galaxies grandissent et évoluent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques comptent utiliser cette nouvelle méthode pour suivre les vents des trous noirs dans d’autres galaxies. Cela les aidera à voir si ce processus est courant et à mieux comprendre comment les trous noirs influencent l’univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles observations détaillées montrent que les vents puissants des trous noirs peuvent chasser le gaz formant les étoiles, ce qui explique pourquoi certaines grandes galaxies ont moins d’étoiles que prévu.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas observaciones muestran cómo los vientos de agujeros negros frenan la formación de estrellas en galaxias gigantes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han preguntado por qué las galaxias más grandes del universo tienen menos estrellas de las esperadas. Una idea es que los agujeros negros supermasivos en el centro de estas galaxias podrían estar impidiendo que se formen nuevas estrellas.

**Qué pasó** – Usando datos del telescopio espacial XRISM, lanzado en 2023, los investigadores estudiaron una galaxia llamada NGC 4151, que está a unos 50 millones de años luz. Encontraron pruebas fuertes de que los vientos que salen de la zona alrededor del agujero negro central soplan el gas necesario para crear nuevas estrellas. Estos vientos son rápidos y pueden expulsar el gas fuera de la galaxia.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ayuda a explicar por qué algunas galaxias gigantes tienen menos estrellas de lo que los científicos esperaban. Al conectar el momento en que el agujero negro emite rayos X con los vientos fuertes, los investigadores entienden mejor cómo los agujeros negros controlan la formación de estrellas. Esto es un gran avance para saber cómo crecen y cambian las galaxias.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar este nuevo método para seguir los vientos de agujeros negros en otras galaxias. Esto les ayudará a ver si este proceso ocurre en más lugares y a mejorar nuestro conocimiento de cómo los agujeros negros moldean el universo.

**Frase clave** – Nuevas observaciones detalladas muestran que los vientos poderosos de los agujeros negros pueden soplar el gas que forma estrellas, explicando por qué algunas galaxias gigantes tienen menos estrellas de lo esperado.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, swirling cosmic vortex symbolizing a supermassive black hole’s accretion disk, with elegant, flowing streams of bright plasma-like winds gently sweeping away delicate clusters of star-shaped lights from a vast, stylized galaxy backdrop rendered in soft, natural blues, purples, and warm golds.

articles.title: We've found a mysterious substance on Titan and Pluto
articles.summary: Something is absorbing light on the surfaces of Pluto and Saturn’s moon Titan, and figuring out what it is could be crucial to understanding Titan’s complex chemistry
articles.content:
Something is absorbing light on the surfaces of Pluto and Saturn’s moon Titan, and figuring out what it is could be crucial to understanding Titan’s complex chemistry

Titan in ultraviolet and infrared wavelengths, as captured by the Cassini probe in 2004 NASA/JPL/Space Science Institute

A strange substance is hiding on the surfaces of both Pluto and Saturn’s moon Titan, and researchers aren’t sure what it is.

Titan’s thick atmosphere means it is extremely difficult to study its surface, so identifying this compound may help us understand the complex chemistry happening there. This moon is one of the most intriguing places in the solar system to search for alien life, and figuring out its chemistry is key to that search.

Astronomers study what distant worlds are made of using spectroscopy: analysing the wavelengths of light that are absorbed, reflected or emitted by chemicals.

Bruno Bézard at the Paris Observatory and his colleagues found the compound using data from the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST). They found a narrow-wavelength band of light being absorbed by something on Titan’s surface, and the same wavelengths of light – although with a broader spread – being absorbed on Pluto.

On the face of it, the two worlds don’t seem alike: Pluto is much colder than Titan, it has no liquid oceans on its surface and its atmosphere is about 15,000 times less dense. But despite all these differences, the chemistry in their atmospheres is similar. “Both atmospheres are mostly nitrogen and methane, so you have, in both, this chemistry in which haze particles are produced and they can snow down and accumulate on the surface,” says Bézard. That is probably how the mystery compound forms, he says.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

The researchers compared the spectral feature seen on both worlds to a myriad of spectra from astronomical observations and laboratory tests, representing compounds that we already know exist in Titan’s atmosphere and forms of ice that might be on both surfaces. None of them matched.

There were a few that were nearly matches, though, which could explain the mystery compound if they were modified slightly or mixed with other molecules. Plus, there would have to be some differences in the form of the substance on Pluto and Titan, perhaps variations in the size of the grains. “We have a few candidates, but it will not be a simple compound,” says Bézard. “Whatever it is, it will be a surprise.”

Investigating further will require a three-pronged approach. First, the researchers have already got more data from JWST, which they say might allow them to track down where the substance is on the surface of Titan. The geological features there might give us some clues. Second, researchers are performing laboratory experiments to test whether the near-matches can be made to fit the mystery spectral feature. And finally, NASA’s Dragonfly spacecraft is scheduled to launch in 2028 and land on Titan’s surface in 2034 – that mission could nail it down once and for all.
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a mysterious substance on Titan and Pluto represents a significant scientific breakthrough that could deepen our understanding of complex chemistry on other worlds, with implications for the search for alien life. The story is focused and provides detailed context about the discovery, its significance, and future research plans, impacting broad scientific knowledge and public interest in space exploration.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover a mysterious substance on Titan and Pluto
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been studying the surfaces of Pluto and Titan, one of Saturn’s moons, to understand their chemical makeup. Both worlds have thick atmospheres made mostly of nitrogen and methane, which create haze particles that settle on their surfaces.

**What happened** – Using the James Webb Space Telescope, researchers found a strange substance that absorbs light in a specific way on both Titan and Pluto. This substance shows up as a unique pattern when studying the light reflected from these surfaces. Although Titan and Pluto are very different worlds, they share this mysterious chemical feature.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it reveals a new chemical compound that scientists did not expect to find. Understanding this substance could help explain the complex chemistry happening on Titan, which is a key place to study when searching for signs of life beyond Earth. It also shows how two very different worlds can have similar chemical processes.

**What’s next step** – Scientists will use more data from the James Webb Space Telescope to pinpoint where this substance is on Titan’s surface. They will also conduct lab experiments to try and identify the compound by comparing it to known chemicals. In addition, NASA’s Dragonfly mission, set to arrive at Titan in 2034, may provide direct information about this mystery.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A newly found substance on Titan and Pluto could unlock secrets about their chemistry and help guide future space missions to better understand these distant worlds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent une substance mystérieuse sur Titan et Pluton
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les surfaces de Pluton et de Titan, une lune de Saturne, pour comprendre leur composition chimique. Les deux mondes ont des atmosphères épaisses principalement composées d’azote et de méthane, qui créent des particules de brume se déposant à leur surface.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb, des chercheurs ont découvert une substance étrange qui absorbe la lumière d’une manière particulière sur Titan et Pluton. Cette substance se révèle par un motif unique lorsqu’on analyse la lumière réfléchie par ces surfaces. Bien que Titan et Pluton soient très différents, ils partagent cette caractéristique chimique mystérieuse.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle révèle un nouveau composé chimique inattendu. Comprendre cette substance pourrait expliquer la chimie complexe de Titan, un lieu clé pour la recherche de signes de vie au-delà de la Terre. Elle montre aussi que deux mondes très différents peuvent avoir des processus chimiques similaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront plus de données du télescope James Webb pour localiser cette substance sur Titan. Ils réaliseront aussi des expériences en laboratoire pour identifier le composé en le comparant à des substances connues. De plus, la mission Dragonfly de la NASA, prévue pour arriver sur Titan en 2034, pourrait fournir des informations directes sur ce mystère.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une substance nouvellement découverte sur Titan et Pluton pourrait révéler des secrets sur leur chimie et guider les futures missions spatiales pour mieux comprendre ces mondes lointains.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren una sustancia misteriosa en Titán y Plutón
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estado estudiando las superficies de Plutón y Titán, una luna de Saturno, para entender su composición química. Ambos mundos tienen atmósferas densas formadas principalmente por nitrógeno y metano, que crean partículas de neblina que se depositan en sus superficies.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb, los investigadores encontraron una sustancia extraña que absorbe la luz de una manera específica tanto en Titán como en Plutón. Esta sustancia aparece como un patrón único al analizar la luz reflejada de estas superficies. Aunque Titán y Plutón son mundos muy diferentes, comparten esta característica química misteriosa.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque revela un nuevo compuesto químico que los científicos no esperaban encontrar. Entender esta sustancia podría ayudar a explicar la química compleja que ocurre en Titán, un lugar clave para buscar signos de vida fuera de la Tierra. También muestra cómo dos mundos muy distintos pueden tener procesos químicos similares.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán más datos del telescopio James Webb para localizar dónde está esta sustancia en la superficie de Titán. También harán experimentos en laboratorio para tratar de identificar el compuesto comparándolo con sustancias conocidas. Además, la misión Dragonfly de la NASA, que llegará a Titán en 2034, podría proporcionar información directa sobre este misterio.

**Frase clave** – Una sustancia recién descubierta en Titán y Plutón podría revelar secretos sobre su química y ayudar a futuras misiones espaciales a entender mejor estos mundos lejanos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned detailed painting of the icy, hazy surfaces of Titan and Pluto side by side, showing swirling nitrogen and methane atmospheres with faint glowing spectral light bands subtly absorbed into the terrain, and abstract crystalline formations symbolizing the mysterious compound shimmering gently under a soft cosmic glow.
